claim.statement: Covid-19–related humor is generally perceived as more aversive (and, depending on format, less funny) than non-Covid humor, and these perceptions are moderated by individual personality traits and psychological distance from the pandemic.
claim.statement_location: Abstract and Discussion sections (PDF pages summarised in the first and last paragraphs of the summary).

data.source: Large-scale online survey of Italian residents during the national lockdown.
data.unit_of_analysis: Individual humor-item rating (participants evaluated multiple jokes).
data.notes: Humor stimuli were collected from public Facebook pages with >10,000 followers; items categorised as Verbal, Meme, or Comic Strip.

method.description: Participants completed an online questionnaire in which they rated Covid-19 and non-Covid humor items for funniness and aversiveness on 7-point scales and answered measures of coping humor, dispositional optimism, demographics, and psychological distance. Ratings were analysed with linear mixed-effects models to test the influence of humor type, personality traits, and psychological distance.
steps: Collect Covid-19 and non-Covid humor items from popular Facebook pages and classify them into Verbal jokes, Memes, and Comic Strips.
Build an online survey presenting each humor item and 7-point funniness and aversiveness scales (0–6).

Include questionnaires: Coping Humor Scale (CHS), Life Orientation Test-Revised (LOTR), perceived infection risk, spatial distance from Codogno, social distance (number of acquaintances infected), and demographics.
Recruit 1,903 adults residing in Italy (and abroad) during lockdown to complete the survey.
Compute scale scores and standardise variables as required.

Fit linear mixed-effects models (lme4 in R) for Funniness and Aversiveness with random effects for participant and item, sequentially adding blocks of predictors (Humor Type, Demographics, Personality, Psychological Distance).
Compare nested models with Likelihood Ratio Tests and report fixed-effect estimates and goodness-of-fit statistics.
method.models: Linear Mixed-Effects Models (using the lme4 package in R) with random intercepts for participants and items.
method.outcome_variable: Ratings of Funniness and Aversiveness (0–6 scale).
method.independent_variables: Humor type (Non-Covid, Covid Verbal, Covid Meme, Covid Strip), Coping Humor Scale score, Dispositional Optimism (LOTR) score, perceived infection risk, spatial distance from Codogno, social distance (known Covid cases).

results.summary: Covid-19 humor was viewed as more aversive than non-Covid humor, though its funniness depended on format: Covid Memes were funniest whereas Covid Comic Strips were least funny. Higher coping-humor and optimism scores increased funniness ratings, especially for Covid jokes, while greater perceived infection risk heightened aversiveness. Spatial distance from the outbreak epicentre increased funniness and lowered aversiveness.
